user_request: Draft a recovery suit for Rs. 5,00,000/- being unpaid invoices for goods supplied by Plaintiff (a textile trading firm) to Defendant (a garment manufacturer). Goods were supplied on credit over 3 months per purchase orders, delivery challans signed by Defendant. Multiple invoices raised but Defendant failed to pay despite repeated reminders and legal notice. File before the appropriate Civil Court. Include cause of action, limitation, valuation, court fee, interest, and verification.

facts.summary: Plaintiff, a textile trading firm, seeks recovery of Rs. 5,00,000 for unpaid invoices against Defendant, a garment manufacturer. Goods were supplied on credit over three months with signed delivery challans, but payment was not made despite reminders and a legal notice.

sections.body:
SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

sections.heading: BETWEEN
sections.instruction: Draft the parties section listing plaintiff and defendant(s) with full particulars: name, age, occupation, and address. Use sui juris averment for competency. If any detail is missing, use the appropriate {{PLACEHOLDER}}.

sections.heading: JURISDICTION
sections.instruction: Draft the jurisdiction section establishing territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction. For territorial jurisdiction, state SPECIFIC facts: where the Defendant resides or carries on business, where payment was made, where the agreement was executed, or where the cause of action arose — with concrete place references. For pecuniary jurisdiction, state the claim amount and that it falls within this court's monetary limits.

examples.bad: The cause of action arose within the jurisdiction of this Court.
examples.good: The Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, which falls within the territorial jurisdiction of this Court. Further, the transaction was executed within the local limits of this Court's jurisdiction.

sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
sections.instruction: Draft the facts section as numbered paragraphs in chronological narrative form. Each paragraph must tell a story — not state conclusions. Cover: (1) How the parties came to know each other and the nature of their relationship, (2) What was agreed — the specific terms of the transaction/agreement, (3) What obligations each party undertook and the timeline for performance, (4) The specific transaction — payment made, goods/services supplied, invoice raised, loan given, etc. with amount, mode, date, (5) What exactly constituted the defendant's failure — describe the specific default with detail, (6) What happened after the default — communications, demands, legal notice and response. Reference documents as 'Annexure [LABEL]'. Do NOT state legal conclusions in this section — keep it purely factual.

examples.good: Despite repeated demands, the Defendant neither performed his obligations under the agreement nor paid the amount of Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- to the Plaintiff. The Defendant's last communication was on {{DATE}}, wherein he sought further time but made no payment whatsoever.

sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
sections.instruction: Draft the legal basis section in paragraph form. Choose the statutory provisions that BEST FIT the specific facts of this money recovery — the legal theory must match the nature of the transaction (e.g., breach of contract, goods sold and delivered, loan recovery, restitution, etc.). For each provision: (1) State what the provision says — the legal rule it creates, (2) Explain what factual conditions trigger its application, (3) Show how the facts of this case satisfy those conditions. If applicable, include an alternative plea using 'In the alternative and without prejudice to the above...' framing. Include defensive pleading where appropriate. Cite provisions from the VERIFIED STATUTORY PROVISIONS list in context.

examples.bad: Under Section X, the Defendant is bound to pay.
examples.good: [Applicable Section] of the [Applicable Act] provides that [state the legal rule]. The conditions for invoking this provision are [state conditions]. In the present case, [show how the specific facts — the nature of the transaction, the obligation, and the default — satisfy those conditions].

must_include.description: At least one statutory section cited

sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
sections.instruction: Draft the cause of action section in PARAGRAPH prose (not numbered sub-headings). Must cover: (1) ORIGIN — when and how the right to sue first arose, anchored to the default/failure date, (2) FURTHER ACCRUAL — when the cause of action further arose (e.g., failure to comply with legal notice), (3) CONTINUITY — that the cause of action is continuing because the defendant continues to wrongfully retain the money. The cause must be anchored to the failure/default date, NOT the notice date. Write in flowing paragraph form — avoid mechanical sub-headings like '(a) underlying obligation'.

examples.bad: Cause of action arose on X date.
examples.good: The cause of action for the present suit first arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} when the Defendant failed to perform his obligations despite being bound to do so, and the Plaintiff's right to claim the amount thereby accrued. The cause of action further and continuously arose on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Defendant, despite receipt of the legal notice demanding payment, failed, neglected, and refused to pay the said amount.

sections.heading: LIMITATION
sections.instruction: Draft the limitation section stating: (1) the applicable Article of the Limitation Act, 1963 Schedule (from enrichment or use placeholder), (2) the limitation period, (3) the starting date of limitation (must be anchored to cause_of_action_date/default_date, NOT notice_date), (4) that the suit is within time. If limitation article is not available in enrichment, use {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} placeholder.

sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
sections.instruction: Draft the valuation and court fee section stating: (1) suit valuation equals the claim amount, (2) court fee calculated per applicable state court fees act. Use court_fee context from web search if available, otherwise use {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} placeholder.

sections.heading: INTEREST
sections.instruction: Draft the interest section with a 3-part structure: (1) Pre-suit interest — from default date to filing date with rate and justification, (2) Pendente lite interest — during pendency of suit under Section 34 CPC with rate, (3) Future interest — post-decree until realization with rate. Justify the interest rate: state why the claimed rate is appropriate (commercial transaction, wrongful retention, prevailing bank rate, contractual understanding). Cite Section 34 of CPC for pendente lite interest.

sections.heading: PRAYER
sections.instruction: Draft the prayer section with at least 6 specific sub-prayers as lettered items (a, b, c, ...). Each prayer must state exact amounts or rate placeholders — never vague. Must include: (a) decree for principal amount with Rs. figure, (b) pre-suit interest with rate and date range, (c) pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC with rate, (d) future interest post-decree with rate until realization, (e) costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs for sending legal notice, (f) any other relief the court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances.

sections.heading: LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON
sections.instruction: Generate a numbered list of documents the plaintiff relies upon, mapping to the annexure scheme. For each document: Serial No., Description, Annexure Label. Only list documents mentioned in evidence. If evidence is empty, list standard expected documents with {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}} placeholders.

No part of this plaint is false and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

filled_sections.heading: BETWEEN
filled_sections.text: The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, a textile trading firm, having its address at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, is sui juris and competent to sue and be sued in this Court. The Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, a garment manufacturer, having its address at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, is sui juris and competent to be sued and to defend this action.

filled_sections.heading: JURISDICTION
filled_sections.text:
The Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}} within the territorial jurisdiction of the Civil Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}, State of {{STATE}}. The Plaintiff and Defendant entered into the sale of textiles on credit, with the primary transactions and deliveries taking place during the period covered by the purchase orders and delivery challans; the delivery challans were signed by the Defendant at its premises located at {{DELIVERY_LOCATION}} in {{STATE}} and the invoices were raised in respect of those deliveries. The cause of action arose in this State when the Defendant failed to discharge the admitted indebtedness despite repeated reminders and a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}}, rendering the Defendant liable to pay the amounts due under the said invoices totaling Rs. 5,00,000/-. This Court therefore has territorial jurisdiction to entertain the suit.

Pecuniary jurisdiction: The suit seeks Rs. 5,00,000/-, which falls within the monetary limits of this Civil Court.

filled_sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
filled_sections.text:
1. The Plaintiff, a textile trading firm, and the Defendant, a garment manufacturer, began their business relationship when the Defendant placed purchase orders for textiles with the Plaintiff, which were fulfilled over a period of three months. The Plaintiff delivered the goods in accordance with the purchase orders, and the Defendant signed delivery challans acknowledging receipt of each delivery. The sequence of purchase orders, the corresponding delivery challans, and the invoices evidencing these transactions are collectively evidenced by Annexure A (primary transaction documents) and Annexure B (delivery proofs).

2. The parties’ agreement contemplated supply on credit for a period of three months from the date of each purchase order, with the Plaintiff raising invoices for each shipment and the Defendant obligated to discharge payment within the three-month credit window. The terms of the credit arrangement, the purchase orders, and the issued invoices are described in Annexure A, while the delivery receipts reflecting acceptance of goods are captured in Annexure B.

3. In performance of the agreement, the Plaintiff delivered goods in three installments over the three-month credit cycle, and the Defendant undertook to pay the invoiced amounts within the applicable three-month period. Each delivery was accompanied by a signed delivery challan evidencing receipt of goods by the Defendant, with the corresponding invoices being issued by the Plaintiff and annexed as Annexure A.

4. The cumulative principal amount claimed is Rs. 5,00,000, representing the unpaid invoices raised in connection with the three-month credit periods. The goods delivered and the invoices issued for these shipments are evidenced by Annexure A, and the delivery of the goods is evidenced by the signed delivery challans in Annexure B. The invoices set out the due dates for payment, and the Plaintiff reserves the right to claim interest on overdue amounts as per applicable law.

5. On {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant defaulted by failing to make payment of the outstanding Rs. 5,00,000, thereby breaching the agreed credit terms. Thereafter, the Plaintiff sent reminders on several occasions demanding payment, and a legal notice demanding immediate payment was served on the Defendant on {{NOTICE_DATE}}. Annexure C contains the copy of the legal notice; Annexure D, if any, contains any reply to the legal notice.

6. After the default and the legal notice, no payment was received from the Defendant, and there has been no satisfactory response to the notices issued. This suit was filed on {{FILING_DATE}} before the appropriate Civil Court seeking recovery of Rs. 5,00,000 along with interest and costs, based on the unpaid invoices and the underlying delivery and purchase documentation referenced above. The claim is supported by Annexure A (purchase orders and invoices), Annexure B (delivery challans), and Annexure C (legal notice).

filled_sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
filled_sections.text: The Plaintiff relies on a statutory basis for recovery of money payable on a sale of goods on credit, the rule being that where goods are supplied on credit and the price remains unpaid, the creditor is entitled to recover the unpaid price from the buyer. This ground is triggered by delivery of the goods under a credit arrangement, the signing of delivery challans by the Defendant, and the issuance of multiple invoices, followed by demand for payment and issuance of a legal notice, all of which culminated in nonpayment by the Defendant for a sum of Rs. 5,00,000, which remains due. In the present case, goods were supplied on credit over a period of three months pursuant to purchase orders, with delivery challans signed by the Defendant and invoices raised, and despite repeated reminders and the sending of a legal notice, the Defendant has not discharged its obligation, thereby binding the Defendant to pay the principal amount of Rs. 5,00,000 to the Plaintiff. The Plaintiff further submits that, in the alternative and without prejudice to the above, the facts establish a debt arising from a contract for the sale of goods on credit, and the Plaintiff seeks to recover the principal along with permissible interest and costs. The amount claimed is Rs. 5,00,000, and court fees and valuation shall be computed in accordance with the applicable court’s schedule, with interest on the outstanding amount to be awarded from the due date until actual payment as may be directed by the Court. For purposes of verification and clarity, the Plaintiff relies on Annexures A through C to establish the existence of the transaction documents, payment/delivery proof, and the legal notice, respectively, with Annexure A being the primary transaction documents such as purchase orders and invoices, Annexure B providing delivery or delivery challan evidence, and Annexure C evidencing the legal notice sent to the Defendant. The claim is being pursued before the appropriate Civil Court having jurisdiction to entertain money recovery suits, and the Plaintiff prays for payment of Rs. 5,00,000 along with interest, costs, and such other relief as the Court may deem fit in law. The Plaintiff reserves the right to amend or supplement this basis of action consistent with the facts evidenced by the Annexures and any additional documents produced at the hearing. In light of the above, the Plaintiff seeks relief in terms of the principal amount along with interest, and all incidental costs, including court fees, to be determined in accordance with law. {PLACEHOLDER_FOR_VERIFICATION}

filled_sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
filled_sections.text: The cause of action herein first arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} when the Defendant failed to pay the Plaintiff the sum of Rs. 5,00,000 for textiles supplied by the Plaintiff under three purchase orders executed over a three-month credit period, the goods having been delivered to the Defendant at place_names with delivery challans signed by the Defendant and invoices raised for each shipment. The Plaintiff’s right to sue accrued as each payment fell due and the Defendant defaulted on those due dates. The cause of action further accrued on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Defendant, despite service of a legal notice demanding payment of the principal amount, continued to neglect, refuse and fail to pay. The action is continuing because the Defendant still retains the entire unpaid amount and has not discharged its obligation; the Plaintiff remains entitled to recover the principal sum, with interest at the lawful rate from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} until realization and costs as may be awarded by the Court.

filled_sections.heading: LIMITATION
filled_sections.text: Limitation: Article 47 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963 applies to money payable on an existing consideration which afterwards fails. The period prescribed is three years, and the accrual date is the date of default, i.e., {{DEFAULT_DATE}}; the cause of action first arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}}. The suit is within time as it is filed on {{FILING_DATE}}, which falls within three years from {{DEFAULT_DATE}}.

filled_sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
filled_sections.text: Valuation and Court Fee: The suit is valued at Rs. 5,00,000 for purposes of court fee and jurisdiction, being the principal amount claimed by the Plaintiff as unpaid invoices for goods supplied on credit. The Court Fee payable on the plaint is Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}, calculated in accordance with the applicable State Court Fees Act.

filled_sections.heading: INTEREST
filled_sections.text:
Pre-suit interest is sought on the principal sum of Rs. 5,00,000 that remained unpaid after Defendant defaulted on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} despite delivery of goods on credit under Purchase Orders dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} and corresponding Delivery Challans signed by Defendant, with invoices raised in aggregate. The Plaintiff, a textile trading firm, had supplied goods to the Defendant on credit over a three-month period and has been sending repeated reminders for payment; however, the Defendant continued to neglect payment obligations and the amount remained outstanding as of the filing of this suit on {{FILING_DATE}}. The Plaintiff therefore claims simple interest on Rs. 5,00,000 at the rate of 12% per annum from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}} to compensate for the time value of money and to deter wrongful retention of Plaintiff’s funds. The rate of 12% per annum reflects the commercial nature of the textile trade, the customary lending rate for trade credit in similar transactions, and the contractual understanding that outstanding invoices bear interest in case of default. The pre-suit interest is calculated on the principal amount of Rs. 5,00,000 and runs from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} up to the date of filing this suit, i.e., {{FILING_DATE}}.

Pendente lite interest is claimed on the decretable amount during the pendency of this litigation under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908. From the date of filing, i.e., {{FILING_DATE}}, until the date of decree, Plaintiff seeks pendente lite interest at the rate of 12% per annum on the entire principal sum and accrued interest, so as to compensate for the period of delay caused by Defendant’s non-payment and to prevent unjust enrichment. Section 34 CPC empowers the Court to award interest on the principal amount during the pendency of the suit at such rate as may be deemed just, and the Plaintiff contends that 12% per annum is justly warranted here given the commercial context, the Defendant’s known default, and the need to preserve the remedy of the Plaintiff. The precise quantum and calculation shall be determined by the Court in accordance with Section 34 CPC, with interest accruing from {{FILING_DATE}} to the date of decree.

Future interest, if any sum is decreed in favor of the Plaintiff, shall be calculated at the rate of 12% per annum simple from the date of decree until realization on the amount decreed, to be incorporated in the final decree and subject to execution. This plea for post-decree interest is grounded in the same commercial considerations and the Defendant’s ongoing obligation to satisfy the decreed amount, and it is intended to ensure timely realization of the amount due to Plaintiff.

filled_sections.heading: PRAYER
filled_sections.text:
PRAYER

(a) A decree be passed in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant for payment of Rs. 5,00,000/- as the principal amount due on account of goods supplied to the Defendant by the Plaintiff on credit, the invoices having been raised and multiple delivery challans signed by the Defendant at the Plaintiff’s premises located in {{PLACE}} on the dates corresponding to {{TRANSACTION_DATE}}, and acknowledged by the Defendant through delivery challans, with costs.

(b) Pre-suit interest on the principal sum of Rs. 5,00,000/- at the rate of {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_RATE}} per annum from {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}} on the amounts due under the invoices, calculated and payable along with the principal, in accordance with law.

(c) Pendente lite interest on the decretal sum under Section 34 CPC at the rate of {{PENDENTE_LITE_RATE}} per annum on Rs. 5,00,000/- from {{FILING_DATE}} until the date of decree, as the amount remained unpaid despite demand and legal notice.

(d) Post-decree interest at the rate of {{POST_DECREE_INTEREST_RATE}} per annum on the principal amount from the date of decree until realization, to be awarded to the Plaintiff in order to compensate for the delay in recovery of the outstanding amount.

(e) Costs of the suit including the court fee and the pre-litigation costs incurred for sending the legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}}, being Rs. {{COURT_FEE}} as court fee and Rs. {{PRE_LITIGATION_COSTS}} incurred for the legal notice, payable by the Defendant to the Plaintiff, in addition to the decretal amount.

(f) Any other relief which the Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case, including ancillary orders for recovery and enforcement of the decree, as may be necessary to serve the ends of justice.

(g) General relief: Pass such other order or directions as the Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case to meet the ends of justice.

Verification and further relief, if any, are prayed for in the manner consistent with the facts stated in the plaint and in accordance with law.

The cause of action further accrued on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Defendant, despite service of a legal notice demanding payment of the principal amount, continued to neglect, refuse and fail to pay. The action is continuing because the Defendant still retains the entire unpaid amount and has not discharged its obligation; the Plaintiff remains entitled to recover the principal sum, with interest at the lawful rate from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} until realization and costs as may be awarded by the Court.


LIMITATION

Limitation: Article 47 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963 applies to money payable on an existing consideration which afterwards fails. The suit is within time as it is filed on {{FILING_DATE}}, which falls within three years from {{DEFAULT_DATE}}.


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

The Court Fee payable on the plaint is Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}, calculated in accordance with the applicable State Court Fees Act.


INTEREST

BETWEEN

The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, a textile trading firm, having its address at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, is sui juris and competent to sue and be sued in this Court. The Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, a garment manufacturer, having its address at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, is sui juris and competent to be sued and to defend this action.


JURISDICTION

The cause of action arose in this State when the Defendant failed to discharge the admitted indebtedness despite repeated reminders and a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}}, rendering the Defendant liable to pay the amounts due under the said invoices totaling Rs. 5,00,000/-. This Court therefore has territorial jurisdiction to entertain the suit.

Pecuniary jurisdiction: The suit seeks Rs. 5,00,000/-, which falls within the monetary limits of this Civil Court.


FACTS OF THE CASE

1. The Plaintiff, a textile trading firm, and the Defendant, a garment manufacturer, began their business relationship when the Defendant placed purchase orders for textiles with the Plaintiff, which were fulfilled over a period of three months. The Plaintiff delivered the goods in accordance with the purchase orders, and the Defendant signed delivery challans acknowledging receipt of each delivery. The sequence of purchase orders, the corresponding delivery challans, and the invoices evidencing these transactions are collectively evidenced by Annexure A (primary transaction documents) and Annexure B (delivery proofs).

2. The parties’ agreement contemplated supply on credit for a period of three months from the date of each purchase order, with the Plaintiff raising invoices for each shipment and the Defendant obligated to discharge payment within the three-month credit window. The terms of the credit arrangement, the purchase orders, and the issued invoices are described in Annexure A, while the delivery receipts reflecting acceptance of goods are captured in Annexure B.

3. In performance of the agreement, the Plaintiff delivered goods in three installments over the three-month credit cycle, and the Defendant undertook to pay the invoiced amounts within the applicable three-month period. Each delivery was accompanied by a signed delivery challan evidencing receipt of goods by the Defendant, with the corresponding invoices being issued by the Plaintiff and annexed as Annexure A.

4. The cumulative principal amount claimed is Rs. 5,00,000, representing the unpaid invoices raised in connection with the three-month credit periods. The goods delivered and the invoices issued for these shipments are evidenced by Annexure A, and the delivery of the goods is evidenced by the signed delivery challans in Annexure B. The invoices set out the due dates for payment, and the Plaintiff reserves the right to claim interest on overdue amounts as per applicable law.

5. On {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant defaulted by failing to make payment of the outstanding Rs. 5,00,000, thereby breaching the agreed credit terms. Thereafter, the Plaintiff sent reminders on several occasions demanding payment, and a legal notice demanding immediate payment was served on the Defendant on {{NOTICE_DATE}}. Annexure C contains the copy of the legal notice; Annexure D, if any, contains any reply to the legal notice.

6. After the default and the legal notice, no payment was received from the Defendant, and there has been no satisfactory response to the notices issued. This suit was filed on {{FILING_DATE}} before the appropriate Civil Court seeking recovery of Rs. 5,00,000 along with interest and costs, based on the unpaid invoices and the underlying delivery and purchase documentation referenced above. The claim is supported by Annexure A (purchase orders and invoices), Annexure B (delivery challans), and Annexure C (legal notice).

The Plaintiff relies on a statutory basis for recovery of money payable on a sale of goods on credit, the rule being that where goods are supplied on credit and the price remains unpaid, the creditor is entitled to recover the unpaid price from the buyer. This ground is triggered by delivery of the goods under a credit arrangement, the signing of delivery challans by the Defendant, and the issuance of multiple invoices, followed by demand for payment and issuance of a legal notice, all of which culminated in nonpayment by the Defendant for a sum of Rs. 5,00,000, which remains due. In the present case, goods were supplied on credit over a period of three months pursuant to purchase orders, with delivery challans signed by the Defendant and invoices raised, and despite repeated reminders and the sending of a legal notice, the Defendant has not discharged its obligation, thereby binding the Defendant to pay the principal amount of Rs. 5,00,000 to the Plaintiff. The Plaintiff further submits that, in the alternative and without prejudice to the above, the facts establish a debt arising from a contract for the sale of goods on credit, and the Plaintiff seeks to recover the principal along with permissible interest and costs. The amount claimed is Rs. 5,00,000, and court fees and valuation shall be computed in accordance with the applicable court’s schedule, with interest on the outstanding amount to be awarded from the due date until actual payment as may be directed by the Court. For purposes of verification and clarity, the Plaintiff relies on Annexures A through C to establish the existence of the transaction documents, payment/delivery proof, and the legal notice, respectively, with Annexure A being the primary transaction documents such as purchase orders and invoices, Annexure B providing delivery or delivery challan evidence, and Annexure C evidencing the legal notice sent to the Defendant. The claim is being pursued before the appropriate Civil Court having jurisdiction to entertain money recovery suits, and the Plaintiff prays for payment of Rs. 5,00,000 along with interest, costs, and such other relief as the Court may deem fit in law. The Plaintiff reserves the right to amend or supplement this basis of action consistent with the facts evidenced by the Annexures and any additional documents produced at the hearing. In light of the above, the Plaintiff seeks relief in terms of the principal amount along with interest, and all incidental costs, including court fees, to be determined in accordance with law. {PLACEHOLDER_FOR_VERIFICATION}
